summary: I started out as a Democrat but became active in the Republican Party in Mississippi around the early 1960s. I first voted Republican for Eisenhower in 1952 and gradually took on leadership roles, becoming county chairman in the early '60s, state finance chairman in '65, and state chairman in '66, serving for about ten years. The party was small and struggling then, often seen as the conservative alternative, but we faced challenges like not fielding candidates in key races, which hurt our growth. Over time, we tried to promote the idea of a two-party system and ideological differences rather than just personality politics. Even though Mississippi voted Republican in presidential elections, the state level remained largely Democratic, but I believed the Republican presence would grow.

I was born into a Democratic family and initially saw southern Democrats as the 'real good guys.' However, I became disillusioned with the party's leadership in Mississippi, seeing many as followers rather than principled leaders. The Democratic Party dominated state politics, and many Mississippians identified with it, often voting based on personality rather than party ideology. In the 1960s and '70s, Democrats still held most state offices, even as the state voted Republican in presidential elections. Figures like John Bell Williams, a conservative Democrat who supported Goldwater, showed the complex nature of the party in Mississippi during that era.

My first vote was for Dwight D. Eisenhower in 1952, which marked my initial support for the Republican Party. Eisenhower represented a new direction for me compared to my Democratic roots. His presidency inspired me to become more politically active, even though I wasn't involved in civic clubs or community leadership before. Eisenhower's election was a significant moment that opened the door for my eventual leadership roles in Mississippi's Republican Party.

I was somewhat involved in Nixon's first campaign in 1960, and later saw him run again in 1968. In the '68 election, Nixon was relatively unknown in Mississippi and actually ran behind George Wallace and Hubert Humphrey, getting the lowest vote in the nation there. Our polls before the election showed Nixon ahead, but Wallace's strong segregationist stance appealed to many voters at the time. Despite this, I believed Nixon could carry Mississippi in a three-way race, and I saw Wallace's support as a kind of stepping stone for voters eventually moving toward the Republican Party.

Rubel Phillips was the Republican candidate for governor in 1963 and again later, and I worked for him during his campaigns. Initially, I opposed his candidacy because I thought his philosophy was too Democratic and not conservative enough. Despite my reservations, he did quite well, getting about 38 to 39 percent of the vote, which was impressive for a Republican in Mississippi at that time. Phillips was seen as a former Truman Democrat and a Rockefeller Republican, which reflected the complex political landscape we faced.

Gil Carmichael ran for statewide office in Mississippi and received about the same percentage of the vote as Rubel Phillips did in his gubernatorial race, around 38 to 39 percent. This showed that despite strong Republican showings in presidential elections, the party struggled to make significant inroads in statewide races. Carmichael's performance highlighted the challenges Republicans faced in growing their vote base beyond national contests.

In 1964, Barry Goldwater was the Republican presidential candidate, and Mississippi gave him enormous pluralities, reflecting strong conservative support. However, the party leadership in Mississippi made the mistake of not running candidates on the ticket to ensure carrying the state, which hurt our growth potential. Goldwater's campaign was a turning point that gave us optimism, though in hindsight, that was somewhat misplaced. His conservative stance resonated with many Mississippians, but the party still struggled to build on that momentum at the state level.

Nelson Rockefeller represented the more moderate wing of the Republican Party during the 1964 primaries, but it was clear early on that Barry Goldwater had the nomination sewn up. At the time, some thought Rockefeller had almost no chance of winning the nomination, which was evident before the delegates even left their homes. This division within the party influenced how we approached the 1964 election in Mississippi, where the conservative Goldwater faction dominated.

John Bell Williams was a conservative Democrat in Mississippi who supported Goldwater and was considered a hero to many Mississippians for that reason. He was removed from his chairmanship of the state commerce commission because of his support for Goldwater, which only increased his popularity. Williams won the governor's race with about 70 percent of the vote, showing how personality and local issues often outweighed party affiliation in Mississippi politics at the time.

George Wallace ran for president in 1968 and carried Mississippi overwhelmingly, even ahead of Nixon and Humphrey. His campaign capitalized on the intense racial issues still present in the state, which made him very popular among many voters. I saw Wallace's vote as somewhat independent, possibly a transitional step for voters who might later support the Republican Party nationally. If Wallace were to run again in 1976 on the Democratic ticket, I believed he would carry Mississippi easily without much effort.

Edmund Muskie was the Democratic candidate in the 1968 presidential race, and our polls showed he ran about 20 percent in Mississippi, behind Wallace and Nixon. Muskie's support was limited in the state, reflecting the challenges Democrats faced in appealing to Mississippi voters during that period. His presence in the race helped split the vote, but ultimately Wallace dominated due to the racial and political climate.

Mississippi has been a challenging political landscape, historically dominated by Democrats but showing strong Republican support in presidential elections since the 1950s. I was active in building the Republican Party here from the early 1960s, trying to promote ideological differences rather than just personality politics. The state voted overwhelmingly for George Wallace in 1968, reflecting the racial tensions of the time. Despite this, I believed the Republican Party would continue to grow in Mississippi, especially in certain areas like Jones County and the coast.

Jones County is one of the areas in Mississippi where the Republican Party has made some inroads. Along with the coast, it represents a pocket where people are more receptive to the idea of a two-party system and ideological distinctions between parties. We have focused efforts there to build the party and recruit candidates, seeing it as part of the broader goal to grow Republican influence in the state.

Alabama's Republican Party took a different approach in 1964 by running candidates on the ticket, which helped them do very well compared to Mississippi. Their willingness to contest races contrasted with Mississippi's mistake of not fielding candidates that year, and it showed the importance of active campaigning. Alabama's experience was a lesson for us in Mississippi about how to build the party and capitalize on conservative support.

The United States Senate races in Mississippi have been tough for Republicans, as the party struggled to field strong candidates and gain significant support in statewide contests. Despite Republican successes at the presidential level, Senate races remained dominated by Democrats during my time in party leadership. We aimed to increase our presence and competitiveness in these races as part of our broader strategy to grow the party.

In Mississippi, Republicans managed to elect at least one congressman to the United States House of Representatives during the 1960s, which was a notable achievement given the party's small size. We believed that with the type of vote we had, we could have elected more, possibly five representatives, but the party's lack of candidates and organization limited our success. Increasing Republican representation in the House was part of our long-term goals for the state.
